section.title: By the Rivers of Babylon
section.sectionRationale: An anonymous communal lament from the Babylonian exile, likely written after the return, this psalm sits among the closing Davidic collection of Book V as the rawest expression of grief and longing for Zion in the whole Psalter, its shocking final imprecation a test case for how honest lament and trust in God's justice belong together.
section.thesis: Grief for Zion refuses to be silenced by mockery, and the psalmist's vow never to forget Jerusalem climbs to a shocking cry for God's own justice against Babylon's documented cruelty, raw lament entrusted to God rather than a license for revenge, and answered at last by Christ, who wept over Jerusalem and prayed for His enemies.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the weeping exiles silenced by their captors' mockery, verses 1-4; the vow never to forget Jerusalem, verses 5-6; and the raw cry for God's justice against Edom and Babylon, verses 7-9.

verses.text: By the rivers of Babylon we sat and wept when we remembered {{tzion|Zion}}.
There on the willows we hung our harps,
for there our captors requested a song; our tormentors demanded songs of joy: "Sing us a song of Zion."
How can we sing a song of the LORD in a foreign land?
If I forget you, O Jerusalem, may my right hand cease to function.
May my tongue cling to the roof of my mouth if I do not {{zakar|remember}} you, if I do not exalt Jerusalem as my greatest joy!

units.label: A cry for God's justice
units.note: the imprecation against Edom and Babylon

verses.text: Remember, O LORD, the sons of Edom on the day Jerusalem fell: "Destroy it," they said, "tear it down to its foundations!"
O Daughter of Babylon, doomed to destruction, {{ashrei|blessed}} is he who repays you as you have done to us.
Blessed is he who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks.

tzion.gloss: Zion, the hill of Jerusalem and its Temple, standing for the place of God's own dwelling among His people. The exiles' grief is grief for God's presence, not merely for a city or a homeland.

zakar.gloss: To remember, to bring to mind in a way that shapes action and loyalty. The psalmist's vow not to forget Jerusalem is a vow of covenant faithfulness, the same word used when the psalm later asks the LORD to remember Edom's cruelty and to act.

ashrei.gloss: Blessed, or how happy, ordinarily an opening word of celebration, as in Psalm 1:1. Here it is used with devastating irony over Babylon's coming downfall, an appeal to God's righteous repayment of documented cruelty, not a celebration of violence for its own sake.

basic.context: Sung by exiles who had seen Jerusalem burned and its people marched seven hundred miles to Babylon, this psalm captures the raw grief of worship interrupted, harps hung silent on the willows, and captors who turned sacred songs into mockery, demanding entertainment from a shattered people.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm moves from lament, to a vow of loyalty to Jerusalem, to an imprecation, a prayer for God's justice against Babylon's cruelty. It describes the exiles' honest anguish and anger; it does not prescribe personal vengeance. The final verse must be read as prayer entrusted to God, echoing the very violence Babylon itself had inflicted, Isaiah 13:16, never as counsel to act it out.
basic.originalLanguages: The exiles wept beside Babylon's canals remembering Tziyon, Zion, the place of God's presence, not just a skyline. The psalmist vows never to zakar, forget, cease to remember Jerusalem, the same root later used to ask the LORD to remember, and act against, Edom's betrayal. The closing ashrei, blessed, is not celebration of cruelty but an appeal that God's justice fall on Babylon exactly as Babylon's cruelty had fallen on Jerusalem.
basic.typology: The exiles' inability to sing the LORD's song in a foreign land anticipates every believer's ache for a homeland not yet fully possessed, Hebrews 11:13-16, while their raw cry for justice against Babylon anticipates the final judgment of every anti-God empire figured as Babylon in Revelation 18.
basic.christ: Where the exiles wept over Jerusalem's fall and cried for vengeance on their enemies, Christ wept over the very same city in its impenitence, Luke 19:41-44, and from the cross prayed for those who crucified Him, Luke 23:34. He bears the curse this psalm cries out for so that both the psalmist's grief and his enemies might find mercy at the cross, without ever softening the psalm's honest hatred of what evil actually does.
basic.god: God is trusted here as the only rightful judge of Babylon's cruelty. The psalm does not ask the exiles to execute vengeance themselves; it hands their unbearable grief and anger to the LORD, trusting Deuteronomy 32:35, Vengeance is Mine, I will repay, and Romans 12:19, which quotes it.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 13:16 describes exactly the atrocity the psalmist prays back upon Babylon. Luke 19:41-44 shows Jesus weeping over Jerusalem rather than cursing it. Romans 12:19 forbids personal vengeance and hands justice to God. Revelation 18:6 answers this psalm's imprecation, repay her as she herself has repaid, at the final judgment of Babylon the great.
basic.oneStory: This psalm is the Bible's rawest lament, exiles grieving a shattered Zion and crying out for God's own justice against documented cruelty. Its unanswered ache for home and its handed-over anger both find their resolution in Christ, who weeps over the city, absorbs the curse, and will one day judge every Babylon and bring His people home for good.

context.literary: A communal lament in three movements, weeping and mockery, verses 1-4; a vow of loyalty sealed by self-curse, verses 5-6; and an imprecation against Edom and Babylon, verses 7-9, moving from present grief to remembered vow to future justice.
context.historical: Composed either during or shortly after the Babylonian exile, 586 to 539 BC, following Nebuchadnezzar's destruction of Jerusalem, 2 Kings 25, and reflecting on the documented brutality of the Babylonians and the treachery of Edom, who cheered Jerusalem's fall, Obadiah 10-14.
context.canonical: Placed among the closing Davidic psalms of Book V, this psalm gives voice to the exile's grief that stands behind so much of the Psalter's language of longing for Zion, and it prepares for the imprecatory honesty found again in Psalms 140 and 143.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give the exiled community words for grief too deep for silence, to vow unbroken loyalty to Jerusalem, and to hand over to God, rather than to human hands, the righteous anger provoked by Babylon's and Edom's cruelty.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes, with unflinching honesty, the emotional reality of exile and the desire for God's justice; it does not prescribe violence against anyone's children. The abiding pattern it prescribes is honest lament brought to God and vengeance entrusted to Him alone.

debates.question: How should believers read the imprecation of verse 9, blessed is he who dashes your infants against the rocks?

views.label: As raw, Spirit-honest prayer appealing to God's justice, using the very language of Babylon's own historically attested atrocities, Isaiah 13:16, and never intended as a call to human action
views.case: The verse addresses God, not Babylon's people directly, and mirrors exactly what Babylon itself had done to Jerusalem's children, asking the righteous Judge to repay cruelty with equivalent cruelty rather than taking matters into human hands.

views.label: As poetic hyperbole expressing the full horror of what Babylon had done, meant to shock hearers into feeling the weight of the atrocity rather than as a literal petition
views.case: Hebrew poetry regularly uses extreme, formulaic language for rhetorical effect, and the verse's purpose may be to make Babylon's own cruelty unbearably vivid rather than to specify a desired outcome.

debates.bearing: Either way, the verse is prayer, not permission. It hands unbearable grief and anger to God's justice rather than authorizing any human being to act it out, and it must always be read alongside Christ's own response to His enemies.

study.originalLanguages: The exiles wept remembering Tziyon, Zion, God's own dwelling, not merely a skyline. They vow never to zakar, forget, cease to remember Jerusalem, the same root by which they ask the LORD to remember, and repay, Edom's betrayal. The closing ashrei, blessed, is a plea for God's justice to match Babylon's own documented cruelty, not a celebration of violence.

typology.type: The exiles' inability to sing the LORD's song in a foreign land, verse 4
typology.fulfillment: Every believer's present ache for a homeland not yet fully possessed, Hebrews 11:13-16, and Philippians 3:20.

typology.type: The cry for justice against Babylon's cruelty, verses 8-9
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment of every anti-God empire figured as Babylon the great, Revelation 18:6.

study.christ: Where this psalm weeps over Jerusalem's ruin and cries for vengeance on Babylon, Christ weeps over the same city in its unbelief, Luke 19:41-44, and prays from the cross for the very people crucifying Him, Luke 23:34. He absorbs the curse this psalm hands to God, so that both the exiles' grief and their enemies can be met with mercy without denying that evil truly deserves judgment.
study.god: God alone is trusted as judge of Babylon's cruelty. The psalm refuses personal vengeance and instead entrusts overwhelming grief and anger to the LORD's own justice, in line with Deuteronomy 32:35 and Romans 12:19.

crossReferences.note: Describes precisely the kind of atrocity the psalmist prays God will bring back upon Babylon, showing the imprecation answers documented cruelty rather than inventing it.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem rather than cursing it, showing how grief over a doomed city is transformed at the cross.
Forbids personal vengeance and commands believers to leave room for God's wrath, the New Testament's own commentary on this psalm's final verses.
Answers this psalm's imprecation directly, repay her as she herself has repaid, at the final judgment of Babylon the great.

study.oneStory: This is the Bible's rawest lament, exiles grieving Zion's ruin and crying for God's own justice against Babylon's cruelty. Their unanswered longing for home and their handed-over anger both find their resolution in Christ, who weeps over the city, bears the curse, and will one day judge every Babylon and bring His people home forever.

sermon.title: Praying Our Anger
sermon.why: Keller works through Psalm 137's searing pain and anger to show what to do with anger over serious mistreatment, both within the psalm's own logic and in light of the cross, making this the clearest trusted-cluster treatment of the psalm's hardest verses.

quiz.q: Why did the exiles hang their harps on the willows rather than sing?

options.text: Their captors mockingly demanded songs of joy, and grief made sacred song feel impossible in a foreign land.
options.note: Verses 2-4 show mockery and grief silencing worship, not mere sadness alone.

options.text: They had forgotten how to play their instruments.
options.note: The text says nothing about forgotten skill; it is grief and mockery that silence them.

options.text: Their harps had been physically destroyed by the Babylonians.
options.note: The harps are hung up, not destroyed; the point is refusal to perform sacred song for mockers.

options.text: They were commanded by God not to sing outside Jerusalem.
options.note: There is no such divine command; the psalm expresses the felt impossibility of singing amid mockery and grief.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist vow in verses 5-6?

options.text: Never to forget Jerusalem, even calling a curse on himself if he fails, showing covenant loyalty to Zion above every other joy.
options.note: The self-curse formula underscores how central Jerusalem was to the psalmist's identity and worship.

options.text: To take up arms and return to fight for Jerusalem immediately.
options.note: The vow is about memory and loyalty, not a call to military action.

options.text: To forgive Babylon completely and never think of Jerusalem's fall again.
options.note: This is the opposite of the text, which vows to remember, not to forget or move on.

options.text: To build a new temple in Babylon instead of Jerusalem.
options.note: The vow is exclusive loyalty to Jerusalem itself, not the founding of an alternative sanctuary.

quiz.q: Against whom does the psalmist specifically ask the LORD to remember wrongdoing in verse 7?

options.text: The sons of Edom, who cheered for Jerusalem's destruction on the day it fell.
options.note: Edom's betrayal, also condemned in Obadiah, is singled out alongside Babylon's cruelty.

options.text: The Assyrians who first threatened Jerusalem generations earlier.
options.note: The psalm addresses Edom and Babylon, contemporaries of the exile, not the earlier Assyrian threat.

options.text: The Egyptians who had enslaved Israel centuries before.
options.note: Egypt is not in view here; the psalm names Edom explicitly regarding Jerusalem's fall.

options.text: The prophets who had warned Judah of coming judgment.
options.note: The prophets are not being accused; the psalm targets nations that acted against Jerusalem, especially Edom.

quiz.q: How is the psalm's shocking final imprecation, verse 9, best understood?

options.text: As raw, honest prayer entrusting God's justice with Babylon's own documented cruelty, never a license for anyone to commit violence.
options.note: The verse is addressed to God as prayer, mirroring exactly the atrocities Babylon itself had inflicted, Isaiah 13:16.

options.text: As a straightforward command for believers to harm their enemies' children today.
options.note: This is a serious misreading; the verse is prayer handed to God, not instruction for human action, and stands against New Testament teaching, Romans 12:19.

options.text: As a later scribal error that should be removed from Scripture.
options.note: The verse is a genuine part of the inspired text and must be read within its lament and imprecatory genre, not excised.

options.text: As proof that the God of the Old Testament is fundamentally different from the God revealed in Christ.
options.note: The same God who hears this cry for justice is the God who, in Christ, both judges evil finally and offers mercy to sinners now.

quiz.q: How does Christ answer the ache and anger of this psalm?

options.text: He weeps over Jerusalem's coming judgment and prays for His enemies from the cross, absorbing the curse while never denying evil deserves judgment.
options.note: Luke 19:41-44 and Luke 23:34 show both grief over the city and mercy toward enemies converging in Christ.

options.text: He ignores the psalm entirely since it does not appear in the New Testament.
options.note: Its themes, weeping over Jerusalem, entrusting vengeance to God, and Babylon's judgment, are all taken up in the Gospels and Revelation.

options.text: He commands His followers to carry out the psalm's imprecation literally against Rome.
options.note: Jesus explicitly forbids retaliatory violence and instead prays for forgiveness for His enemies.

options.text: He promises that Jerusalem will never again face judgment of any kind.
options.note: Jesus in fact predicts further judgment on Jerusalem, Luke 19:43-44, even while weeping over it in love.

questions: The exiles could not sing the LORD's song in a foreign land. Where do you feel that same tension between grief and worship right now?
The psalmist vows never to forget Jerusalem above his greatest joy. What competes with Christ for the place of your greatest joy?
This psalm hands overwhelming anger over injustice to God rather than acting it out. What anger are you carrying that needs to be honestly prayed rather than either suppressed or acted upon?
Christ wept over the very city that would reject Him, and prayed for those who crucified Him. How does His example reshape the way you pray about people who have wronged you or those you love?
Revelation 18 promises that every Babylon will finally be judged. How does trusting God's future justice change how you carry present grief and anger today?

translationNotes.note: The BSB reads willows, with a footnote noting the Hebrew may also mean poplars, trees that commonly grew beside Babylon's canals and were well suited for hanging up unused instruments.
May my right hand cease to function renders a Hebrew idiom for the hand losing its skill or withering, a self-curse invoked if the psalmist ever forgets Jerusalem.

The verse mirrors, almost word for word, the kind of documented atrocity ancient conquerors like Babylon inflicted, Isaiah 13:16, so the prayer asks for measure-for-measure divine justice rather than inventing a new cruelty.
